topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Subject line: "Update Regarding Professor Albright's Departure." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly above the keys. Albright. My entire academic trajectory, the scaffolding for my entire Master's thesis, was built around his guidance. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, squeezing the air from my lungs. I reread the message, the words blurring into an abstract threat. What was I supposed to do? The deadlines were looming, the data collection was halfway complete, and now the entire structure felt suddenly, terrifyingly, hollow. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, the cheap carpet muffling the frantic rhythm of my footsteps. I kept glancing at the clock; every second felt stretched thin, agonizingly slow.

[story 2]

"He's actually leaving?" I whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. My hands were slick with sweat as I gripped my laptop, the screen reflecting back my own wide, startled eyes. The realization hit me with the force of a physical impact. All the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, all the painstaking revisions of Chapter Three—it was all suddenly adrift. A sharp intake of breath escaped me, followed by a shallow, rapid series of breaths. I started frantically shuffling through my notes, rifling through binders, searching for an escape route, a contingency plan that simply didn't exist. My jaw felt locked, my vision tunneling slightly at the edges. I needed a lifeline, and I couldn't even see where to grab it.

[story 3]

I stared at the university website, the official announcement mocking me with its bureaucratic calm. Albright was gone. The silence in my head was deafening, a vacuum where direction used to reside. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven drumbeat demanding attention. I slammed my fist softly onto the desk, the vibration jarring my arm. I began to pace the length of my small apartment, running a hand through my already disheveled hair. Every thought cycled through a rapid-fire sequence of 'what ifs' and 'how do I fix this?' The reality of facing the next few months without his oversight washed over me, leaving a chilling sense of exposure. I stopped dead in front of the mirror, my breath catching in my throat, feeling utterly unmoored.
